summary: Juniper Street in Midtown is shut down for at least six weeks for emergency sewer repairs. Atlanta United picked up their first W of the season in style. A former USPS worker copped to stealing cash from mail at an Atlanta facility. And an Atlanta hairstylist just got an Oscar nomination for her work on 'Sinners.'

topStory.headline: Juniper Street Shut Down for Emergency Sewer Repairs, At Least 6 Weeks
topStory.summary: Juniper Street in Midtown is closed for at least six weeks for emergency sewer repairs. Drivers need to reroute through Piedmont or West Peachtree. Midtown businesses along the corridor are bracing for the disruption.
topStory.body:
If you use Juniper Street to cut through Midtown, time to update your route. The city's Department of Watershed Management shut it down for emergency sewer line repairs, and they're saying at least six weeks. Engineers flagged the failing line during recent inspections and apparently it couldn't wait. The detour routes are Piedmont Avenue and West Peachtree Street, which are already busy enough. MARTA rail isn't affected, but some bus routes along the corridor might shift slightly. Check MARTA's site for updates if you ride the bus through that stretch. The real pain here is for Midtown businesses on Juniper that depend on street-level foot traffic and visibility. Six weeks of construction barriers and detour signs is no joke for a coffee shop or restaurant trying to catch the lunch crowd. The city says crews will work to keep sidewalk access open where possible, but it's going to be ugly for a while.
Alternate routes: Piedmont Ave (east) or West Peachtree St (west). Expect heavier traffic on both through at least late April.
topStory.imageAlt: Emergency sewer repair work on Juniper Street in Midtown Atlanta

stories.headline: Atlanta United Grabs First Win of the Season, 3-1 Over Philly
stories.summary: Mercedes-Benz Stadium was rocking Saturday as Atlanta United beat Philadelphia Union 3-1 for their first win of the 2026 season. The Five Stripes looked like a different team.
stories.body:
Atlanta United supporters finally had something to celebrate Saturday night. The Five Stripes beat Philadelphia Union 3-1 at Mercedes-Benz Stadium for their first win of the 2026 MLS season. The supporters section was loud from the opening whistle, and the team actually looked like they wanted to be out there. After a start to the season that had Twitter calling for the coach's head (the usual), Saturday was a reminder that this roster has talent when it clicks. The attack looked fluid, the defense actually held a shape, and the Benz had that energy that makes you remember why you bought season tickets.
Next home match is March 21 vs. Charlotte FC. Tickets available at atlutd.com.
stories.imageAlt: Atlanta United fans celebrate at Mercedes-Benz Stadium

stories.headline: Atlanta Hairstylist Scores Oscar Nomination for 'Sinners'
stories.summary: Shunika Terry, an Atlanta hairstylist, earned an Oscar nomination for her work on 'Sinners,' which received a record 16 nominations. A spotlight moment for Atlanta's massive film industry.
stories.body: Atlanta's own Shunika Terry is heading to the Academy Awards. She earned a nomination for her hair and makeup work on Sinners, which pulled a record-breaking 16 Oscar nominations this year. Terry built her career in Atlanta's booming film industry and has been doing hair for productions in the city for years. This is a big deal for Atlanta's production scene. The state's film tax credit program has turned Georgia into one of the biggest filmmaking hubs in the world (yes, the world), and moments like this show that the talent behind the camera here is world-class, not just the tax incentives. Terry's nomination joins a growing list of Oscar recognition tied to Georgia productions. Whether she takes home the statue or not, this is the kind of story that proves Atlanta's film industry has depth beyond being "Hollywood South."
stories.imageAlt: Atlanta hairstylist Shunika Terry, Oscar nominee for Sinners

stories.headline: Mayor Throws Punches Instead of Signing Firefighter Contract
stories.summary: Atlanta's mayor is getting into public spats instead of signing the firefighter union contract. The union has been waiting months. Meanwhile, the department keeps losing people to better-paying suburban departments.
stories.body:
The mayor's office is picking public fights instead of finalizing the firefighter union contract, according to reporting from Atlanta Civic Circle. The firefighters union has been at the negotiating table for months trying to lock down better pay and working conditions, and members are running out of patience. This matters because Atlanta Fire Rescue has been struggling with recruitment and retention. Experienced firefighters can get better deals in other metro departments or leave for the private sector entirely. Every month the contract sits unsigned is another month the city risks losing institutional knowledge that takes years to replace.
Context: Atlanta firefighters start around $42,000/year. Neighboring departments like Cobb County and DeKalb County offer higher starting pay with better benefits packages. The union is pushing for pay parity.
stories.imageAlt: Atlanta mayor and firefighter union contract dispute

stories.headline: Georgia House Passes Bill to Open Up Medical Cannabis Access
stories.summary: The Georgia House approved a bill to fix the state's medical cannabis program, which has been mostly broken since it was authorized in 2015. It now goes to the governor.
stories.body: The Georgia House passed a bill this week that would actually make the state's medical cannabis program functional. The legislation, sponsored by Augusta Republican Rep. Mark Newton, cuts through the red tape that's kept Georgia's program from working since it was first authorized back in 2015. Georgia technically legalized medical cannabis oil over a decade ago, but the rollout has been a mess. Patients with qualifying conditions still struggle to access products through the tiny number of licensed dispensaries. This bill would expand access points and simplify the licensing process so patients don't have to drive hours to get their medication. The bill heads to the governor's desk next. It passed with bipartisan support in the House, which is a good sign. Neighboring states like Florida have built much more functional medical cannabis programs, and advocates have been pointing to that gap for years.
stories.imageAlt: Georgia House chamber during medical cannabis vote

stories.headline: Former USPS Workers Plead Guilty to Stealing Cash from Atlanta Mail
stories.summary: Two former postal workers admitted to stealing cash and gift cards from mail at an Atlanta USPS facility. Both pleaded guilty in federal court and await sentencing.
stories.body: Two former U.S. Postal Service employees pleaded guilty in federal court this week after admitting they stole cash and gift cards from mail at an Atlanta facility. The scheme went on for months before postal inspectors caught on. The pair targeted letters and packages that looked like they might contain cash or gift cards. Low-tech, high-volume. Federal prosecutors say this kind of mail theft is unfortunately not rare, and they encourage anyone who suspects tampering to file a report with the U.S. Postal Inspection Service. Both defendants are awaiting sentencing. If you've been wondering why grandma's birthday card arrived empty, well, now you have at least one possible explanation.

stories.headline: Nine Georgia Chefs Named to 2026 Georgia Grown Executive Chef Class
stories.summary: Nine Georgia chefs were named to the 2026 Georgia Grown Executive Chef class, recognized for championing locally sourced ingredients from Georgia farms.
stories.body:
Nine chefs from across the state were inducted into the 2026 Georgia Grown Executive Chef class at a ceremony at the state capitol on March 11. The program recognizes chefs who go out of their way to source ingredients from Georgia farms and producers. If you care about where your food comes from (and if you're reading a local news site, you probably do), the Georgia Grown designation is worth paying attention to. These are kitchens that are actively building relationships with Georgia farmers rather than ordering everything off a truck from a national distributor.
The full list of 2026 Georgia Grown Executive Chefs is available at georgiagrown.com.
stories.imageAlt: Georgia Grown Executive Chef ceremony at the state capitol

stories.headline: Alliance Theatre Reveals Kendeda Playwriting Prize Winner and Finalists
stories.summary: The Alliance Theatre announced the winner of the Kendeda Playwriting Prize. The winning play gets a full production at the Woodruff Arts Center in Midtown.
stories.body: The Alliance Theatre announced the winner of its Kendeda Playwriting Prize this week. The prize is one of the most prestigious new play awards in the country, and the winning play gets a full production at the Alliance's Woodruff Arts Center campus in Midtown. The Kendeda Prize has been a launching pad for plays that go on to major runs across the country. Previous winners have hit Broadway and top regional theaters. For Atlanta's theater community, it's proof that the city is a place where new work gets developed, not just a touring stop for shows that already premiered somewhere else. Production details and casting announcements are expected in the coming weeks. Check alliancetheatre.org for updates.

stories.headline: Tulsa Welding School Opens Atlanta Campus as Trade Jobs Boom
stories.summary: With roughly 400,000 skilled trade jobs sitting unfilled nationally, Tulsa Welding School just opened an Atlanta campus. The trades shortage is projected to hit 2 million by 2033.
stories.body:
Tulsa Welding School just opened an Atlanta campus, betting on the metro area's massive demand for skilled trades workers. The numbers back them up: roughly 400,000 skilled trade jobs are unfilled nationally, and that number is projected to hit nearly 2 million by 2033. The Atlanta campus offers welding and HVAC programs for students who want to skip the four-year degree path. Starting salaries for certified welders in the Atlanta metro regularly top $50,000, with experienced welders earning significantly more. In a city with as much construction happening as Atlanta, there's no shortage of work.
The trades gap in numbers: 400,000 unfilled positions today, projected 2 million by 2033. Average certified welder salary in Atlanta metro: $50,000+. More info at tws.edu/campus/atlanta.
